text:
Governments will always be predisposed towards tax-and-spend.
It takes either an extreme financial/fiscal crisis, or a government with a strong sense of ideology/discipline to prod governments towards the straight and narrow path of fiscal prudence. Not to mention how hard it is to root out entrenched interests (public employee payrolls are a bulging low-hanging fruit, but good luck to those who try to prune it).

Even our last Conservative government (despite a good start) was not immune.
And the Liberal governments of the 90s adopted fiscal conservatism out of necessity.

For those of us who question the efficacy of government efforts/spending to spur the economy, or the benefits of heavy-handed government intervention into individuals' lives and the market, the only logical solution is to put more power into the hands of the individual citizen by shrinking government. Good luck with that.

text:
Oh, woe! The U.S. is in such a divisive state! 

Good news, I say. People have a right to protest and now feel free to do so (they didn't always...very few U.S. protests before mid-20th century.) And now--thank you, mass media and electronic communication--the protests have real clout. About time its people rose up (although, as usual, their champion is deeply flawed and--worse, this time--may actually represent the people the "masses" despise: the wealthy 1%. The greatest irony of all.)

But it didn't start with Vietnam. It didn't really start with Civil Rights movement. It may have started in 1860, when (a few) people began talking about "Robber Barons"--the phrase gained wide for the first time and referred to Cornelius Vanderbilt, and later to other wealthy 19th and early-20th century industrialists who got rich on the back of workers. The only outlets: a few newspapers and magazines that relatively few people saw let alone read.

text: Re RSP, this is incorrect. Your RRSP is really 2 accounts -one is yours, one is the governments. If you have $100,000 in a RRSP, and or marginal tax rate is 50%, you have $50000 and the government has $50000 (remember the deduction you got when you put the money in?). In your account, all the growth is tax free and you pay no tax when you take your money out of your RRSP. You do pay back the governments account so it feels as though you are paying tax, but you are really just paying back the government the money it lent you plus growth. Therefore, paying no taxes on the growth of your money is better than paying capital gains and getting the dividend tax credit.

text:
To Jim, 

National Parks support naturally regulating eco-systems which allow wildlife to regulate their own populations with as little human influence as possible. NPS is not allowed to manipulate wildlife populations for abundance or favor one species over another. 

This allows scientific study of an unaltered system which can then be used to compare to heavily managed systems outside the parks. This provides a baseline for a variety of scientific studies. 

Regarding adding additional entrances into the park - first this would take significant govt spending in the hundreds of millions of dollars (probably much more) when NPS already has an 11 billion dollar maintenance backlog among all National Parks, & it would take years and possibly decades to complete & would be incredibly disruptive to the current & future tourism seasons.

Domesticating Denali through more development is not the answer; protecting what we have & supporting the park that contributes so much to Alaska - is.
label: non-biased

text: I will dispute a couple points. First off, driving is a privilege, not a right. Interstate transport is a right, but it can be done without a car. Second, if the lawsuit is based on state law, the school zone is outside the limitations given within the law, therefore the school zone, and thus the speed limit, are unlawful. This is what the case is about. Unlawful laws do not need to be heeded and are unenforceable. Again, this is also at the heart of the lawsuit: Is the placement of the school zone, at ~1,000 feet away from Saghalie, an unlawful placement.\n\nIn other words, if the speed limit is reduced to 20 MPH due to the unlawful placement of a school zone, then any tickets for doing between 21 and 35 MPH should be thrown out, refunded, and removed from the driver's record. The school zone should also be relocated to a valid area or removed entirely.

text:
"

The short answer is: no. The tow maneuvers look almost identical from outside the cockpit, but from inside it is clear that there is a very distinct difference. In an aileron roll, you pull up on the stick and then push it forward again (going back o a neutral position), and then roll with the ailerons using no elevator. It's the aerial version of ""popping a wheelie."" 

In a slow roll, you may be rolling as fast as possible or you may take a full ten minutes to roll the plane. In this case, you have to use both rudder and elevator during the roll to keep on course, or else you'll find yourself in a nose die towards the ground. An aileron roll is fairly easy for a beginner, but a slow roll takes a great amount of practice even for an expert. An aileron roll is clumsy an uncoordinated, but extremely fun, while a slow roll is precise and difficult. An aileron roll will also keep the g-force positive, while a slow roll turns you all around like clothes in a dryer. "
label: non-biased
